description: This figure presents the mechanical properties of two low-alloy ultrahigh-strength steels (0Al and 1.3Al, differing in aluminum content) in the as-quenched (WQ) and tempered conditions (180°C for 2 h). Panel (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves: in the as-quenched state, the 0Al steel exhibits an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~2294 MPa with 9.5% total elongation (TE), while the 1.3Al steel shows ~2145 MPa UTS but fractures prematurely at ~4.3% strain before reaching maximum strength. After tempering, both steels show reduced UTS (~1925 MPa for 0Al and ~1876 MPa for 1.3Al) but significantly improved ductility (TE increases to 13.5% and 13.7%, respectively). Panel (b) shows Charpy U-notch impact absorbing energy (KU₂): the 0Al steel improves from 33 ± 6 J to 65 ± 3 J after tempering, whereas the 1.3Al steel demonstrates a dramatic toughness enhancement from 23 ± 2 J to 80 ± 3 J (approximately 3.5-fold increase), achieving superior impact resistance compared to the 0Al counterpart at comparable strength levels (~1.9 GPa). These results collectively demonstrate that the introduction of high-density NiAl clusters through tempering enables exceptional strength-toughness synergy in the 1.3Al steel.
